request.question: Name one of Bob Dylan's most-performed live songs, that is said to be inspired by the Prospect Park Water Tower in Minneapolis.

title: Kenwood Park Water Tower
Park Slope
Butler Square
Harry W. Jones House
Washburn Park Water Tower
All Along the Watchtower
Highland Park Tower
Prospect Park, Minneapolis
Prospect Park Water Tower
Old Chicago Water Tower District
sentences: The Kenwood Park Water Tower is an octagonal brick and stone water tower in the Kenwood neighborhood of Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States.
It was built in 1910 and designed by Frederick William Cappelen, the city of Minneapolis engineer at the time.
The tower is 110 feet tall, making it the tallest structure in Kenwood.
The tower has not been used to store water since 1954.
The tower was built to alleviate water pressure and storage problems in the Lowry Hill area.
Although the tower is not the work of a master architect or representative of a specific architectural style, its design is distinctive.
It is ornamented with projecting ribs, narrow rectangular windows, and Lombard bands, suggesting a medieval fortress.
The tower serves as a distinct visual focus within the neighborhood.
Park Slope is a neighborhood in northwest Brooklyn, New York City.
Park Slope is roughly bounded by Prospect Park and Prospect Park West to the east, Fourth Avenue to the west, Flatbush Avenue to the north, and Prospect Expressway to the south.

Generally, the section from Flatbush Avenue to Garfield Place (the "named streets") is considered the "North Slope", the section from 1st through 9th Streets is considered the "Center Slope", and south of 10th Street, the "South Slope".
The neighborhood takes its name from its location on the western slope of neighboring Prospect Park.
Fifth Avenue and Seventh Avenue are its primary commercial streets, while its east-west side streets are lined with brownstones and apartment buildings.
Butler Square (originally the Butler Brothers Company building) is a former warehouse and office building in Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States.
The building is located within the Minneapolis warehouse district and was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1971.
It is significant for its restrained Chicago School design by major Minneapolis architect Harry Wild Jones, and as a leading example of the older warehouse/office buildings in Minneapolis–Saint Paul.
Jones designed other buildings in Minneapolis such as the Minneapolis Scottish Rite Temple, Calvary Baptist Church, the Lakewood Cemetery Chapel, and the Washburn Park Water Tower.
The Harry W. Jones House is a historic house in Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States, in the Tangletown neighborhood.
Harry Wild Jones was one of the most notable architects in Minneapolis, with a career spanning nearly 50 years.
His designs included the Butler Brothers Warehouse, the chapel in Lakewood Cemetery, and the Washburn Park Water Tower.
His own home, built in 1887, was one of the first homes in the Washburn Park area and influenced the design of other homes in the area.
The house is listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

The Washburn Park Water Tower poses as a landmark of early 20th-Century architectural achievement within the Tangletown neighborhood in south Minneapolis, Minnesota, and has been doing so for nearly 75 years.

Perched on top of one of the highest points in south Minneapolis, the tower is given the privilege to boast its unique location and role as an unofficial "beacon" for incoming planes landing at Minneapolis-Saint Paul International Airport, yet remains hidden from much of the residents and visitors that pass by the base of the hill each day.

This is mainly because large homes and tall oak trees scatter the hillside where the tower resides, and even more so, because of the clustered mess of streets and dead ends that compromise the towers' occupancy.
Hence the name, 'Tangletown'.
"All Along the Watchtower" is a song written and recorded by American singer-songwriter Bob Dylan.
The song initially appeared on his 1967 album "John Wesley Harding", and it has been included on most of Dylan's subsequent greatest hits compilations.
Since the late 1970s, he has performed it in concert more than any of his other songs.
Different versions appear on four of Dylan's live albums.
The Highland Park Water Tower is a water tower in the Highland Park area of Saint Paul, Minnesota.
It was designed by Clarence W. Wigington, the nation's first African-American municipal architect.
The tower was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1986.
It was completed in 1928 at a cost of $69,483.
Prospect Park is a historic neighborhood within the University community of the U.S. city of Minneapolis, Minnesota.

The area is bounded by the Mississippi River to the south, the City of Saint Paul, Minnesota to the east, the Burlington Northern railroad yard to the north, and the Stadium Village commercial district of the University of Minnesota to the west.
The neighborhood is composed of several districts which include the East River Road area.
The 1913 Prospect Park Water Tower is a landmark and neighborhood icon.

The Prospect Park Water Tower, sometimes referred to as the "Witch's Hat Water Tower" or just "The Tower", is a historic water tower in the Prospect Park neighborhood of Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States.
It was built in 1913 on Tower Hill Park, a hilltop park established in 1906.
The water tower has become the neighborhood's architectural mascot for its singular design by Frederick William Cappelen.
The tower is rumored to be the inspiration for Bob Dylan's song "All Along the Watchtower," as the tower was clearly visible from Dylan's home in nearby Dinkytown.
The Old Chicago Water Tower District is a historic district along the Magnificent Mile shopping district in the Near North Side community area of Chicago, Illinois.
The district is located on both sides of North Michigan Avenue between East Chicago and East Pearson Streets.
It includes the Chicago Water Tower, Chicago Avenue Pumping Station, and Chicago Fire Department Fire Station No. 98.
All three structures are part of the Chicago Landmark district designated on October 6, 1971 (amended June 10, 1981).
The Water Tower and Pumping Station were jointly added to the National Register of Historic Places on April 23, 1975.
In addition the Tower was named an American Water Landmark in 1969.
The Water Tower was also one of the few buildings to survive the Great Chicago Fire.
The district is the namesake of the nearby Water Tower Place.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: Name one of Bob Dylan's most-performed live songs, that is said to be inspired by the Prospect Park Water Tower in Minneapolis.
hop1_docs: Prospect Park Water Tower | The Prospect Park Water Tower, sometimes referred to as the "Witch's Hat Water Tower" or just "The Tower", is a historic water tower in the Prospect Park neighborhood of Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States.  It was built in 1913 on Tower Hill Park, a hilltop park established in 1906.  The water tower has become the neighborhood's architectural mascot for its singular design by Frederick William Cappelen.  The tower is rumored to be the inspiration for Bob Dylan's song "All Along the Watchtower," as the tower was clearly visible from Dylan's home in nearby Dinkytown.

As I Went Out One Morning | "As I Went Out One Morning" is a song written by Bob Dylan, released on his 1967 album "John Wesley Harding".  He has only performed this song live once, in the early phase of the Bob Dylan and The Band 1974 Tour.

Prospect Park, Minneapolis | Prospect Park is a historic neighborhood within the University community of the U.S. city of Minneapolis, Minnesota.  The area is bounded by the Mississippi River to the south, the City of Saint Paul, Minnesota to the east, the Burlington Northern railroad yard to the north, and the Stadium Village commercial district of the University of Minnesota to the west.  The neighborhood is composed of several districts which include the East River Road area.  The 1913 Prospect Park Water Tower is a landmark and neighborhood icon.

Shelter from the Storm | "Shelter from the Storm" is a song by Bob Dylan, released on his 15th studio album, "Blood on the Tracks", in 1975.  Along with "Tangled Up in Blue", "Shelter from the Storm" was one of two songs from "Blood on the Tracks" to be re-released on the 2000 compilation "The Essential Bob Dylan".  The song also appears on two live albums by Bob Dylan — "Hard Rain" (from a May 1976 performance) and "At Budokan" (recorded in February 1978).  A first take of the song, from the same recording session that produced the album track, is included on "The Best of Bob Dylan, Vol.  1" (1997).

All I Really Want to Do | "All I Really Want to Do" is a song written by Bob Dylan and featured on his Tom Wilson-produced 1964 album, "Another Side of Bob Dylan" ("see" 1964 in music).  It is arguably one of the most popular songs that Dylan wrote in the period immediately after he abandoned topical songwriting.  Within a year of its release on "Another Side of Bob Dylan", it had also become one of Dylan's most familiar songs to pop and rock audiences, due to hit cover versions by Cher and the Byrds.

The 30th Anniversary Concert Celebration | The 30th Anniversary Concert Celebration is a live double-album release in recognition of Bob Dylan's 30 years as a recording artist.  Recorded on October 16, 1992, at Madison Square Garden in New York City, it captures most of the concert, which featured many artists performing classic Dylan songs, before ending with three songs from Dylan himself.

All Along the Watchtower | "All Along the Watchtower" is a song written and recorded by American singer-songwriter Bob Dylan.  The song initially appeared on his 1967 album "John Wesley Harding", and it has been included on most of Dylan's subsequent greatest hits compilations.  Since the late 1970s, he has performed it in concert more than any of his other songs.  Different versions appear on four of Dylan's live albums.
hop1_titles: As I Went Out One Morning
All I Really Want to Do
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: Prospect Park Water Tower — alleged inspiration for a Bob Dylan song.

- Prospect Park Water Tower: also called the "Witch's Hat Water Tower" or "The Tower"; "built in 1913." (passage [1])
- Inspiration claim: the tower "is rumored to be the inspiration for Bob Dylan's song 'All Along the Watchtower,'" and "was clearly visible from Dylan's home in nearby Dinkytown." (passage [1])
- Song facts: "'All Along the Watchtower' ... initially appeared on his 1967 album 'John Wesley Harding'." (passage [7]) And "Since the late 1970s, he has performed it in concert more than any of his other songs." (passage [7])
- Ambiguity/missing facts: no alternative candidate song appears in the passages; the inspiration claim is labeled a "rumor" (citation/source for the claim is not included here). Bridge entity: the song "All Along the Watchtower" (connects tower → Dylan live-performance claim).
- Next-hop retrieval (priority):
  1. "All Along the Watchtower" — fetch article lede and sections "Live performances" / "History" to confirm the statement and supporting citations.
  2. "Prospect Park Water Tower" — fetch page section "History" or "In popular culture" to view source for the "rumored" inspiration claim.
  3. "Bob Dylan" — check lede/infobox or "Touring" / "Live performances" sections for lists of most-performed songs and corroborating stats.
- Conclusion: passages provide the necessary linkage; "no additional retrieval needed" to identify the candidate song for answering, but retrieve the above pages to verify citations before producing the final answer.
response.hop2_query: All Along the Watchtower live performances history citations
hop2_docs: All Along the Watchtower | "All Along the Watchtower" is a song written and recorded by American singer-songwriter Bob Dylan.  The song initially appeared on his 1967 album "John Wesley Harding", and it has been included on most of Dylan's subsequent greatest hits compilations.  Since the late 1970s, he has performed it in concert more than any of his other songs.  Different versions appear on four of Dylan's live albums.
All Along the Watchtower (TV series) | All Along the Watchtower is a British sitcom that aired on BBC One in 1999, about an RAF site in Scotland, it was written by Pete Sinclair and Trevelyan Evans.

Live (T.S.O.L. album) | Live is a live album by the American rock band T.S.O.L. (True Sounds of Liberty), recorded January 17, 1988 at The Coach House in San Juan Capistrano, California and released later that year through Restless Records.  It includes performances of songs from the band's three prior studio albums—"Change Today? " (1984), "Revenge" (1986), and "Hit and Run" (1987)—as well as two cover songs, one of The Doors' "Roadhouse Blues" and one of Bob Dylan's "All Along the Watchtower".  Founding guitarist Ron Emory left the band after this album, leaving bassist Mike Roche as the only remaining original member.

Recently (EP) | Recently is an EP by the Dave Matthews Band, released in 1994.  All songs are live recordings.  "Recently" is an edit of the live version found on "Remember Two Things", recorded at The Flood Zone in Richmond, VA on August 10, 1993; "Dancing Nancies" and "Warehouse" were recorded at The Birchmere in Alexandria, Virginia on February 21, 1994; "All Along the Watchtower" and "Halloween" were recorded at Trax in Charlottesville, Virginia on February 22, 1994.

Road Rock Vol. 1 | Road Rock Vol.  1: Friends & Relatives is a live album released in 2000 by Neil Young.  The "friends and relatives" include Ben Keith, Chrissie Hynde, Duck Dunn, Young's then wife, Pegi, and his sister, Astrid.  The album features an unreleased song, "Fool For Your Love", which dates from Young's "This Note's for You" period and a Bob Dylan cover, "All Along the Watchtower."

Watchtower (song) | "Watchtower" is the fifth single by British rapper Devlin, and the lead single from his second studio album, "A Moving Picture" (2013).  The song features British singer-songwriter Ed Sheeran and was produced by Labrinth.  "Watchtower" was released on 16 August 2012 and entered the UK Singles Chart on 26 August 2012 at number 7, becoming Devlin's highest-charting single and first top 10 hit and Sheeran's fifth top 10 hit.  The song samples the main chorus line from Bob Dylan's "All Along the Watchtower".  It also borrows Jimi Hendrix's riff from the guitarist's cover.  It was used in the Denzel Washington and Mark Wahlberg 2013 action-comedy film, "2 Guns".  The instrumental was also used as the main theme for the series "The Young Pope" by Paolo Sorrentino.

The Watchtower | The Watchtower Announcing Jehovah's Kingdom is an illustrated religious magazine, published monthly by Jehovah's Witnesses via the Watch Tower Bible and Tract Society of Pennsylvania.  Along with its companion magazine, "Awake! ", Jehovah's Witnesses distribute "The Watchtower—Public Edition" in their door-to-door ministry.
response.summary_2: The tower "is rumored to be the inspiration for Bob Dylan's song 'All Along the Watchtower,'" and "was clearly visible from Dylan's home in nearby Dinkytown." Bob Dylan wrote and recorded "All Along the Watchtower" and has performed it in concert more than any of his other songs since the late 1970s.
